atomicEnergy.position: Head of Division, Corresponding Member of the NAS of Ukraine, Doctor of Technical Sciences, Senior Researcher
atomicEnergy.name: Volodymyr Ivanovych Borysenko
biography: Volodymyr Ivanovych Borysenko graduated from the Faculty of Physics of Taras Shevchenko Kyiv State University with a degree in Experimental Nuclear Physics. He began his professional career in 1983 as a control physicist at the thermal physics laboratory of Rivne NPP. After completing postgraduate studies at Taras Shevchenko Kyiv State University in 1987, he worked at the Institute for Nuclear Research of the NAS of Ukraine until 1996, progressing from junior researcher to head of department. From 1996 to 1999, he served as Deputy Director for Research at the State Scientific and Engineering Centre for Control Systems and Emergency Response. From 1999 to 2002, he headed a department at the Institute for NPP Operational Support. Since 2003, he has worked at ISP NPP, initially as Head of the Safety Management Systems Department and, since 2016, as Head of the Division for Nuclear Power Engineering.

During his scientific career, V. I. Borysenko has led many large-scale technical projects, including the creation and implementation of safety parameter display systems at 11 Ukrainian VVER-1000 power units; adaptation and application of the RELAP-3D calculation code for VVER safety analysis; development of applied software for neutron flux monitoring systems; and other projects.

He teaches at the Department of Nuclear Physics of the Faculty of Physics at Taras Shevchenko National University of Kyiv, delivering the specialist courses Nuclear Physics Aspects of Nuclear and Thermonuclear Reactors, Nuclear Reactor Physics, Nuclear Reactor Calculation Methods, Transient Processes in Nuclear Power Installations, and Nuclear Reactor Dynamics.
atomicEnergy.publications: The total number of scientific publications exceeds 100.
atomicEnergy.nuclearInstallationsSafety: Department for Safety of Nuclear Installations
directions: development of algorithms and software for determining the neutron-physical parameters of nuclear reactors, fuel-containing materials, and other multiplying systems;
research into the reliability of electrical and thermal equipment at power units;
analysis of thermohydraulic parameters for safe NPP operation;
research and forecasting of environmental contamination during NPP accidents;
development of scientific foundations and methods for environmental remediation.
atomicEnergy.resultsIntro: The departments within the Division have carried out the following work:
results: analog and digital reactivity meters have been developed and are in operation;
applied software for calculating reactor period and reactivity has been developed and implemented;
methods for noise analysis of neutron detector signals have been developed to determine reactor neutron-physical parameters;
models have been developed to study the criticality of fuel-containing materials at the Shelter facility;
a methodology for comprehensive analysis of NPP generating-equipment failures has been developed;
an optimal power-unit structure has been proposed, accounting for expanded turbogenerator reactive-power control ranges;
a methodology for calculating three-dimensional temperature fields of the TVV-1000-2UZ turbogenerator has been developed;
environmental contamination during NPP accidents has been studied and forecast using various dispersion models;
the secondary resuspension and transport of radioactive aerosols in the near-surface atmosphere have been studied and modelled;
scientific foundations and methods for environmental remediation have been developed;
a system for rapid monitoring of the burnup depth of spent fuel assemblies has been developed.

nuclearInstallationsSafety.portraitPlaceholder: Reserved for the department head’s portrait
structure: Thermal Hydraulics of Nuclear Installations Sector
activities: Development of theoretical foundations and experimental-data processing software for measuring subcritical characteristics of nuclear systems using neutron-noise methods.
Development and optimization of new reactor concepts, including accelerator-driven systems and nuclear-fission wave reactors.
Modelling the transmutation of radioactive waste in a subcritical reactor.
Research into special basalt-fibre-based materials for nuclear power engineering.
Investigation of pre-accident heat and mass transfer and hydrodynamic processes in safety-critical systems of nuclear power units.
Computer simulation of thermophysical and thermohydraulic processes during normal, pre-emergency and emergency operating conditions.
Development of mathematical models, algorithms and software for operational diagnostics of nuclear power-unit equipment.
Creation of intelligent systems for early automatic recognition of potentially hazardous equipment conditions.

sectors.title: Reactor Physics Sector
sectors.head: Head of Sector — Kostiantyn Cherevko, Doctor of Technical Sciences
paragraphs: The sector developed a two-group neutron-noise theory based on random branching processes, enabling the analysis of systems with different neutron spectra. Methods for processing experimental data and accounting for external noise were also developed.

A hardware and software system was created to record and analyse neutron noise in nuclear materials. Neutron-noise diagnostic methods are being introduced at Ukrainian NPPs to measure fuel-assembly burnup.

Research covers new reactor concepts, remote antineutrino diagnostics, travelling-wave reactors and subcritical reactors. The sector participates in an IAEA project studying accelerator-driven subcritical systems.

sectors.title: Thermal Hydraulics of Nuclear Installations Sector
sectors.head: Head of Sector — Igor Sharaevsky, Doctor of Technical Sciences, Senior Researcher
paragraphs: The team investigates pre-accident thermophysical processes in safety-critical nuclear power-unit equipment, including reactor cores, turbogenerators and main circulation pumps.

The sector develops mathematical models, intelligent algorithms, and software and hardware systems for automatic recognition of operational anomalies, improved in-core monitoring, and operator information support.
results: The reliability of calculation correlations for heat-transfer crises and coolant boiling onset was assessed.
Flow-regime maps for steam-water structures used by the RELAP-5/MOD3 thermohydraulic code were verified.
Algorithms and software for diagnosing, monitoring and forecasting the condition of NPP heat-and-power equipment were developed and tested.

radiationEcology.headPosition: Head of Department, Doctor of Technical Sciences, Senior Researcher
biography: Mykola Talerko graduated from the Faculty of Physics of Taras Shevchenko National University of Kyiv in 1976. In 2011, he defended his doctoral dissertation in Environmental Safety.
He taught courses at the Department of Meteorology of Taras Shevchenko National University of Kyiv. He has co-authored two monographs and has about 100 published works.
directions: Assessment of environmental radioactive contamination caused by possible accidental NPP releases using atmospheric radionuclide-transport modelling.
Development and implementation of computer decision-support systems for emergency radioactive releases at nuclear power plants.
Development of atmospheric radionuclide-transport models.
Modelling air pollution caused by forest fires in radioactively contaminated areas.
tasks: Reducing uncertainty in radiation-situation assessments during emergencies while accounting for the ecological characteristics of contaminated areas.
Improving contamination forecasts by coupling a regional atmospheric radioactivity-transport model with the WRF numerical weather-prediction model.
Improving radioecological monitoring to select countermeasures that minimize public radiation exposure.

sectors.title: Sector for Studies of Radioactive Aerosol Transport in the Atmosphere
sectors.head: Head — Viktor Shynkarenko, PhD in Physical and Mathematical Sciences, Senior Researcher
directions: Autoradiography of hot particles and determination of their beta activity.
Research into the dissolution kinetics of hot particles in biological-fluid simulants.
Physical modelling of aerosol flows and particle behaviour in ecosystems.
Analysis of radioactive fallout in the Chornobyl NPP zone and Kyiv region.

sectors.title: Sector for Complex Analysis and Database Management
sectors.head: Head — Tetiana Lev, PhD in Geographical Sciences, Senior Researcher
directions: Comprehensive radiation-situation assessment using GIS and databases.
Preventive assessment of radioecological criticality in areas affected by NPPs.
Operational aerosynoptic forecasts using the WRF model.
Mapping, geospatial databases, and software-based data analysis.

sectors.title: Sector for Nature Management and Rehabilitation
directions: Justification of protective measures in radioactively contaminated areas.
Long-term radiation monitoring, dose assessment, and radionuclide-migration modelling.
Creation of cartographic data banks and analysis of satellite information.
Research into the behaviour of metals and radionuclides in the soil-plant system.

researchAreas.title: Atmospheric Transport of Radioactive Contaminants
researchAreas.description: Radioactive aerosols at the Shelter facility and in the Chornobyl exclusion zone were studied, resuspension parameters were consolidated, and a long-term volumetric-activity forecasting method was developed.

researchAreas.title: Atmospheric Transport Models
researchAreas.description: The LEDI model was created to assess emergency releases and transboundary transport over distances up to 1,000 km; the WRF weather model and emergency-response information databases were adapted.

researchAreas.title: Radionuclide Migration in Food Chains
researchAreas.description: Migration of ¹³⁷Cs and ⁹⁰Sr in soil-plant and soil-plant-animal-product systems was studied, and the corresponding models were developed and verified.

researchAreas.title: Rehabilitation of Contaminated Areas
researchAreas.description: The effectiveness of protective measures following radiation accidents was consolidated, and agrochemical countermeasures for contaminated agricultural land were scientifically justified.

researchAreas.title: Emergency Response and Monitoring
researchAreas.description: Emergency-response guidance, a preventive radioecological zoning methodology, and methods for establishing emergency monitoring networks in contaminated areas were developed.

cooperation: Development of mesoscale transport models for radioactive contaminants and other toxic substances.
GIS analysis of environmental conditions and statistical processing of spatial data.
Creation of geospatial information databases on territorial contamination.
Mapping for radioecological zoning and identification of critical areas.
Preparation of representative synoptic-situation datasets for scenario calculations.
Planning radioecological monitoring networks for agricultural areas.

radiationNuclearTechnologies.name: Volodymyr Mykhailovych Rudko
biography: Volodymyr Rudko graduated from the Kyiv Civil Engineering Institute in 1975 with a degree in Industrial and Civil Engineering. He began his career at the State Research Institute of Building Structures, working as an engineer, junior researcher, and researcher.

From 1992, he participated in studies of the technical condition of the Shelter facility’s structures. In 1994, he headed the newly created Department of Building Structures, a joint unit of the ISTC Shelter and the State Research Institute of Building Structures. In 1995, he became Head of the Division for Design of Facilities with Radiation and Nuclear Technologies at ISP NPP.
He participated in mitigating the consequences of the 1986 Chornobyl NPP accident. He has more than 150 scientific publications and is a co-author of seven monographs and two patents.
radiationNuclearTechnologies.achievements: Awarded the Order of Merit, Third Class; the Certificate of Honour of the Verkhovna Rada of Ukraine; and an award from NNEGC Energoatom.

departments.title: Department for Safety Analysis of Radiation-Hazardous Facilities
Department of Building Technologies and Structures

directions: Development of licensing documents as part of design documentation for facilities using radiation and nuclear technologies.
Development and scientific substantiation of design, technical, radiation-hygiene, and organizational measures that ensure acceptable radiation safety for personnel and the environment.
Scientific and technical support for Chornobyl NPP decommissioning, safe operation of the Shelter facility, and its transformation into an environmentally safe system.
Selection and justification of optimal technologies and equipment for safe radioactive-waste and spent-nuclear-fuel management.
Environmental research in the impact areas of the Shelter facility and other radiation-hazardous facilities.
results: Together with scientific, design, and construction organizations, the Division developed and successfully implemented the Shelter facility structural-stabilization project. This work increased facility safety and protected people and the environment against structural collapse and the associated radiological consequences.

The Division made an important contribution to the overall strategy for the New Safe Confinement and its feasibility study. Since 2005, its specialists have provided scientific and technical support for NSC design and construction and developed a support programme for commissioning and operation.
radiationNuclearTechnologies.projectsTitle: Safety analyses and environmental impact assessments were completed for
projects: Reconstruction of the Chornobyl NPP main building, Units 3 and 4, including strengthening and sealing structures that form the NSC enclosure.
The new ventilation stack for Chornobyl NPP Stage II.
Reconstruction of the Unit 4 turbine-hall enclosure at Chornobyl NPP.
The Chornobyl NPP dry spent-fuel storage facility, ISF-2.
Final shutdown and preservation of Chornobyl NPP Units 1, 2, and 3.
The centralized spent-fuel storage facility for Ukrainian NPPs with VVER reactors.
Decommissioning of the Chornobyl NPP cooling pond.
Dismantling the metal reinforcement truss above the Shelter facility’s southern roof.

nuclearRadiationSafety.name: Viktor Oleksandrovych Krasnov
biography: Viktor Krasnov was born in Kyiv on 29 January 1949. He graduated from the Faculty of Chemical Technology of Kyiv Polytechnic Institute in 1972 with a degree in Chemical Cybernetics and from the Faculty of Radiophysics of Taras Shevchenko Kyiv State University in 1982 with a degree in Laser Engineering.
He began his career at the Arsenal plant in Kyiv, progressing from engineer to head of department. In February 1993, he joined the ISTC Shelter and progressed from engineer to Head of Division.

He organizes and leads research and development work on the Shelter facility’s nuclear and radiation safety, fuel-containing materials, remote technologies, monitoring, and international projects. He has authored four monographs and more than 100 scientific and technical articles and served on an international expert group on severe-accident management.
nuclearRadiationSafety.awards: Awarded the Medal for Labour Distinction, the Order of Merit, Third Class, and Certificates of Honour from the Verkhovna Rada of Ukraine and the Presidium of the NAS of Ukraine.

departments.title: Department of Radiation Materials Science and Radiation Instrumentation

directions: Research into the Shelter facility to forecast its nuclear, radiation, and radioecological safety, including radiochemical analysis and alpha-gamma spectrometry of samples.
Study of the physicochemical properties, radionuclide content, and elemental composition of fuel-containing materials and their interaction with the environment.
Research into the form, spatial arrangement, and geometry of fuel-containing material clusters and forecasting their condition and behaviour.
Development and implementation of remotely controlled technologies for radiation-hazardous work at the Shelter facility and other nuclear facilities.
Scientific support for transforming the Shelter facility into an environmentally safe system.
Development of methods for monitoring nuclearly hazardous materials with uncertain mass and geometry parameters.
Analysis of neutron activity, gamma-radiation dose rate, and temperature using the Shelter facility’s standard and research systems.
Modelling neutron-physical characteristics and forecasting the subcritical state of nuclearly hazardous materials.
Adaptation of international unmanned-technology experience and development of remotely controlled equipment prototypes.
Physicochemical, radiochemical, and spectrometric research into radioactive substances and nuclear-fuel-containing materials.
Resolution of technological, technical, and radiation-environmental issues in radioactive-waste management.
Experimental studies of irradiated nuclear fuel and fuel-containing materials exposed to accident conditions.
Expert assessment of methods and technologies for managing materials containing irradiated fuel.
Scientific and technical support for construction, commissioning, and operation of the New Safe Confinement.
nuclearRadiationSafety.resultsIntro: The Division has completed extensive scientific and technical work under contracts with Chornobyl NPP and international customers, including priority Shelter Implementation Plan projects.

achievements.title: Modernized Dust Suppression System
achievements.description: The radioactive-aerosol suppression system beneath the destroyed Unit 4 roof was commissioned and scientifically supported. A continuous polymer coating over almost all material surfaces significantly improved radiation conditions inside the Shelter facility and in the surrounding environment.

achievements.title: Spatial Gamma-Radiation Field Assessment
achievements.description: An aerostat was used to measure exposure dose rates at the NSC Arch construction site at elevations up to 120 metres. The results helped optimize staffing and the required radiation-protection measures.

achievements.title: Radioactive-Waste Radiation Monitoring Complex
achievements.description: A rapid method for characterizing solid radioactive waste and procedures for monitoring and processing measurements were developed; the RMMT complex was metrologically certified and commissioned.

achievements.title: Nuclear and Radiation Safety Monitoring Systems
achievements.description: Systems monitoring neutron flux, gamma radiation, and alpha and beta aerosols near fuel-containing material clusters and throughout Shelter facility rooms were metrologically certified and commissioned.

atomicEnergyStandard.position: Head of Division, Corresponding Member of the NAS of Ukraine, Doctor of Technical Sciences, Senior Researcher
atomicEnergyStandard.name: Volodymyr Borysenko
biography: He graduated in Experimental Nuclear Physics from Taras Shevchenko Kyiv State University. He worked at Rivne NPP, the Institute for Nuclear Research, and scientific centres supporting NPP operation.
He joined ISP NPP in 2003 and has headed the Division for Nuclear Power Engineering since 2016. He led major projects involving safety parameter display systems and reactor safety calculation codes.
He teaches specialist courses in nuclear reactor physics, calculation, and dynamics at Taras Shevchenko National University of Kyiv.

biography: The department combines the Reactor Physics Sector and the Thermal Hydraulics of Nuclear Installations Sector. Its work covers neutron-physics diagnostics, new reactor concepts, thermohydraulic safety, and operational monitoring of nuclear power-unit equipment.

Thermal Hydraulics of Nuclear Installations Sector — Head Igor Sharaevsky

Intelligent operational diagnostic tools for power-unit equipment.

Created a hardware and software complex for neutron diagnostics of nuclear materials.
Researches travelling-wave and subcritical reactors in international projects.
Developed models and algorithms for automatic recognition of operational anomalies.
Verified thermohydraulic models and NPP equipment condition forecasting tools.

biography: He graduated from the Faculty of Physics of Kyiv National University in 1976 and defended his doctoral dissertation in Environmental Safety in 2011.
He taught atmospheric physics at Taras Shevchenko National University of Kyiv. He co-authored two monographs and about 100 scientific works.

hazardousFacilitiesSafety.name: Leonid Pavlovskyi
biography: He graduated from the All-Union Correspondence Polytechnic Institute in 1985 with a degree in Nuclear Power Plants and Installations. He has worked at ISP NPP since 1996 and has headed the department since 2015.
Before and after the 1986 accident, he worked as a senior reactor control engineer at Chornobyl NPP. He participated in the accident response and the post-accident restart of Units 1 and 2.
His work improved radiation-field research under highly anisotropic gamma conditions and introduced ALARA-based radiation-protection approaches. He co-authored a patent and more than 50 publications.
hazardousFacilitiesSafety.highlight: Awarded the Order of Merit, Third Class, government certificates, and departmental distinctions.
directions: Research and forecasting of radiation conditions at facilities using radiation and nuclear technologies.
Development of organizational, technical, and radiation-hygiene protection measures for normal operation and potential accidents.
Preparation of safety analysis and sanitary-compliance reports.
Environmental impact assessment and measures to minimize radiological effects.
results: Developed an angular gamma-intensity measurement system, the Ekran shielding facility, a collimated gamma spectrometer, and specialized dosimeters.
Completed comprehensive radiation surveys in Shelter transformation work areas.
Prepared regulator-approved safety and environmental reports for structural stabilization, the ventilation stack, and NSC enclosure projects.
Provided scientific support for the first NSC commissioning complex.
Substantiated radiation safety for storage construction, Buriakivka disposal-site preservation, and cooling-pond decommissioning.
workCaptions: Biological shielding modelling with the Ekran facility

nppDecommissioning.position: Head of Department, PhD in Engineering, Senior Researcher
nppDecommissioning.name: Valerii Derenhovskyi
biography: He graduated with honours from Taras Shevchenko Kyiv University in 1995 as a mathematician and also qualified as a radioecologist at the Ukrainian Radiological Training Centre.

He worked at the Prypiat association and Chornobylinterinform agency. He joined ISP NPP in 1998 and progressed from junior researcher to head of department. He also studied at the Abdus Salam International Centre in Trieste.
In 2019, he defended a dissertation on multicriteria environmental-safety analysis for facilities using radiation and nuclear technologies.
nppDecommissioning.highlight: Author of 40 scientific publications and one monograph.
directions: Analysis of international NPP decommissioning experience and recommendations on strategies and protection of workers, the public, and the environment.
Preparation of licensing materials, safety analysis reports, environmental assessments, and sanitary-compliance reports.
Recommendations for safe radioactive-waste and spent-fuel management and technical support for storage facilities.
Proposals for Chornobyl NPP decommissioning, fuel-containing material removal, and Shelter transformation.
results: Completed the environmental assessment for final shutdown and preservation of Chornobyl NPP Units 1–3.
Supported NSC design in radioactive-waste management.
Prepared recommendations for decommissioning waste programmes and improvements to the national waste-management system.
Developed technical solutions for removal, sorting, containerization, and storage of Shelter fuel-containing materials.
Established background radiation levels at the centralized spent-fuel storage site.
workCaptions: Model of the fuel-containing material removal area
Background measurements at the centralized spent-fuel storage site

buildingTechnologies.name: Oleh Balan
biography: He graduated from the Kyiv Civil Engineering Institute in Industrial and Civil Engineering and has worked at ISP NPP since 2004.
He specializes in construction planning and technologies for nuclear and thermal power facilities and participated in projects at Khmelnytskyi and Rivne NPPs and Kyiv CHP.
buildingTechnologies.highlight: Participant in the response to the Chornobyl NPP accident.
directions: Safe construction, installation, and dismantling technologies for the Shelter and other radiation-hazardous facilities.
Assessment and forecasting of structural condition.
Three-dimensional modelling and visualization of planned work.
Radiohydrogeological monitoring at Chornobyl NPP and in the exclusion zone.
results: Assessed structures, equipment, and engineering systems at the Shelter and Chornobyl Unit 3.
Surveyed structures at the Pidlisnyi radioactive-waste site and Chornobyl-2 antenna facilities.
Implemented safe-work and shielding solutions under difficult radiation conditions.
Modelled dismantling of unstable Shelter structures in three dimensions.
Established groundwater and contaminated-water monitoring and NSC hydrogeological forecasting.
workCaptions: Three-dimensional modelling of Shelter truss dismantling
Groundwater field research at the NSC site

radiationMonitoring.position: Head of Department, PhD in Engineering
radiationMonitoring.name: Valerii Khan
biography: He graduated from the Moscow Institute of Chemical Engineering and earned his PhD in 1992.

He has worked at ISP NPP since 1997, progressing from engineer to head of department. Since June 1986, he has participated in the Chornobyl accident response and the development of treatment processes and pilot installations.
His research covers physicochemical and radiation properties of fuel-containing materials, liquid radioactive waste and aerosols, and treatment of contaminated water.
radiationMonitoring.highlight: Author of more than 100 publications and five patents; awarded a Certificate of Honour by the Verkhovna Rada of Ukraine.

Laboratory of Physicochemical Analysis — O. O. Odintsov
Laboratory of Spectrometric Analysis — O. K. Kalynovskyi
directions: Monitoring methods for radiation-hazardous facilities and their surroundings.
Comprehensive assessment of Shelter radiological impacts.
Environmental protection measures during Shelter transformation.
Radiochemical analysis and alpha and gamma spectrometry of air, water, soil, and fuel-containing material samples.
Research into the composition, location, condition, and behaviour of fuel-containing materials.
results: Completed radiation surveys and protection substantiation for SIP projects.
Developed polymer formulations for radioactive-aerosol suppression.
Commissioned and calibrated the in-vehicle radioactive-waste monitoring complex.
Studied conditions for spent-fuel storage construction and cooling-pond decommissioning.
Regularly monitors aerosols, radioactive water, and fuel-containing material degradation.
Operates radiochemical, spectrometric, radiometric, and X-ray fluorescence laboratories.

radiationMaterials.position: Head of Department, Doctor of Physical and Mathematical Sciences
radiationMaterials.name: Serhii Habelkov
biography: Born on 10 April 1961 in Balakliia, he graduated from the Faculty of Physics and Technology of Kharkiv State University in 1984. He spent 25 years at the Kharkiv Institute of Physics and Technology, ultimately heading its radiation-resistant ceramics laboratory.
He joined ISP NPP in 2012. He earned his candidate degree in 1996 and doctoral degree in 2014.
The department studies mechanical, thermophysical, electrical, magnetic, and other properties of fuel-containing materials, especially lava-like materials at the Shelter facility.
radiationMaterials.highlight: Co-author of 147 scientific works, one monograph, and six patents and inventor certificates.
directions: Physics of radiation damage in ceramic and glass-ceramic materials.
Study of irradiated structural and reactor-core materials exposed to accident conditions.
Research into current properties of irradiated fuel and NSC-Shelter fuel-containing materials.
Monitoring fuel-containing materials as a fundamentally non-equilibrium system.
Solid-phase conditioning methods for long-term storage or disposal of lava-like materials.
results: Improved understanding of nuclear-material processes during severe accidents.
Assessed methods for managing irradiated-fuel-containing materials.
Prepared recommendations for managing radioactive NSC-Shelter materials.
Studied pore formation, uranium oxidation, cracking, and degradation of fuel-containing materials.
Continues monitoring and medium-term forecasting of fuel-containing material condition.

nuclearSafety.position: Acting Head of the Nuclear Safety Department
nuclearSafety.name: Oleksandr Mykhailov
biography: He graduated from the Moscow Engineering Physics Institute in 1988 as a physics engineer. He worked at the Kurchatov Institute of Atomic Energy and the Research Centre for Radiation Medicine.
From 2001 to 2009, he helped implement automated radiation-monitoring systems at Ukrainian NPPs and the Shelter facility and individual dosimetry systems. He joined ISP NPP in 2010.
His research covers the composition of nuclearly hazardous fuel-containing material clusters in room 305/2, automated monitoring data, and radionuclide-vector methodology.
nuclearSafety.highlight: Author of 95 scientific publications, more than 40 published while working at ISP NPP.

Modelling chemical composition and neutron-physical properties of clusters in room 305/2.
Algorithms for determining subcriticality and preventive criticality suppression.
Monitoring concrete temperature and microclimate inside the Shelter.
Assessment of protective polymer coating and radioactive-aerosol generation.
Development of a Shelter digital twin.
Uncrewed ground and aerial systems for remote surveys.
Support for solid radioactive-waste characterization using radionuclide vectors.

Supported active testing of the Chornobyl solid radioactive-waste processing plant.
Implemented standards for polymer-coating assessment and radionuclide vectors.
Assessed consequences of a possible self-sustaining fission chain reaction in NSC-Shelter.
Developed hazardous-subcriticality detection algorithms and alarm setpoints.
Forecast NSC-Shelter nuclear safety using MCNP-6.2.

humanResources.name: Iryna Pohrebniak
humanResources.portraitAlt: Iryna Pohrebniak, Head of Human Resources
biography: Iryna Pohrebniak has headed Human Resources at ISP NPP since February 2012. She began working in human resources at the Special Design and Technology Bureau of the Institute for Nuclear Research in 1979 and headed that department for 10 years.

The Institute's personnel policy balances workforce renewal and retention while supporting staff development, professional qualifications, the prestige of scientific work, social protection, and employee motivation.

This policy helps form a highly qualified team capable of addressing environmental safety, safe and efficient NPP operation, decommissioning, radioactive-waste management, measurement under NPP conditions, and the development of NPP systems and instruments.

sections.title: Responsibilities and Functions
items: Building a stable workforce and strengthening labour discipline and public order.
Maintaining personnel records and analysing staff composition.
Managing recruitment, transfers, dismissals, and employee leave.
Preparing personnel, disciplinary, and employee-recognition orders.
Maintaining employment records, personnel files, and employee cards.
Supporting certification of management, engineering, technical, and scientific staff.
Preparing government reports and analysing current personnel information.
Maintaining the organization's military records.
Preparing employee pension documents.
Processing authorization documents for employees working at nuclear installations and with nuclear materials.
Processing access permits for the 30-kilometre exclusion zone and Chornobyl NPP Unit 4.

safetyQualityLabor.name: Andrii Levchenko
safetyQualityLabor.portraitAlt: Andrii Levchenko, Head of the Radiation Safety, Quality and Occupational Safety Department
biography: He graduated in 1983 as a mechanical engineer specializing in machinery and equipment for food production.

In 1998, he qualified as a thermal-power physics engineer in nuclear power plants at the NTUU KPI Institute of Postgraduate Education. In 2007, he completed a master's degree in project management at the KNUCA Institute of Postgraduate Education.

He began his career at Chornobyl NPP in 1985 and progressed from auxiliary turbine-equipment operator to project and programme manager. From 26 April 1986, he participated in the response to the Chornobyl NPP accident.

From 2005 to 2019, he worked in the Shelter Implementation Plan project-management unit as a deputy project manager, project and programme manager for the Integrated Automated Monitoring System, and lead engineer for the New Safe Confinement project.
He joined ISP NPP in December 2019 and progressed from lead engineer to head of department.

Monitoring and analysing quality-management performance under ISO 9001:2015.

legalService.name: Volodymyr Serheiev
biography: He graduated from the Academy of Advocacy of Ukraine in 2003 with a specialist degree in law.
He joined the Institute for Safety Problems of Nuclear Power Plants of the National Academy of Sciences of Ukraine in 2019 as Chief Legal Counsel.

sections.title: Activities
items: Organizing the work of the Legal Service.
Drafting and analysing legal documents and ensuring their compliance with current legislation.
Organizing claims and litigation work.
Ensuring legal compliance and analysing and concluding contracts.
Representing ISP NPP before national and local government bodies.
Representing ISP NPP before law-enforcement, judicial, notarial, and administrative bodies with all rights granted by law to claimants, defendants, and third parties.

planningProduction.name: Nataliia Masiuk
planningProduction.portraitAlt: Nataliia Masiuk, Head of the Planning and Production Department
biography: She graduated from the Chernihiv State Institute of Economics and Management in 2006 with a specialist qualification in finance.
From 2004, she worked as a chief state auditor-inspector at the Ivankiv State Tax Inspectorate.
She joined ISP NPP in 2021 as a lead accountant in the Accounting Service and has headed the Planning and Production Department since 2023.

sections.title: Activities
items: Economic planning for the Institute's scientific programmes.
Organizing and supporting all Institute contracting activities.
Budgeting.
Preparing the Institute's expenditure estimates.
Optimizing the organizational structure and staffing plan.
Improving employee remuneration arrangements.
Comprehensive economic analysis of the Institute's operations.

accountingService.name: Yuliia Balyberdina
biography: She graduated in accounting and auditing from the Hetman Petro Konashevych-Sahaidachnyi Kyiv State Academy of Water Transport.

From 2001 to 2013, she worked at the Ivankiv District State Administration's Department of Labour and Social Protection as a specialist, chief specialist, and head of department. From 2013 to 2016, she was chief treasurer at the State Treasury Service office for Ivankiv District, Kyiv Region.
She joined ISP NPP in 2016 and progressed from lead economist to Chief Accountant.

The Accounting Service records financial and economic activity, prepares reports, provides complete and reliable information about transactions and results, supports the management of budget allocations and resources, and ensures compliance with budget legislation.

sections.title: Functions
items: Accounting for the Institute's funds, settlements, income, and expenses.
Controlling and recording all business transactions in the accounting system.
Preparing the Institute's financial statements.
Providing management with complete, accurate, and impartial information on financial position, performance, and cash flows.
Conducting inventories and checking accounting practices in Institute departments.
Creating or accepting primary accounting documents.
Systematizing document information and preparing interim calculations for transaction accounting.
Monitoring the availability and movement of funds.
Monitoring fixed assets and production inventories.
Reviewing primary settlement documents involving suppliers, customers, and accountable persons.
Recording fixed-asset and inventory transactions involving assets received from the NAS of Ukraine material and technical supply administration.
Preparing and submitting monthly and quarterly reports to statistical and fiscal authorities.
Calculating and paying salaries and sick-leave benefits on time.

officeDepartment.name: Iryna Klizhevska
biography: Iryna Klizhevska joined ISP NPP in March 2002 and worked as deputy office manager for all Shelter Implementation Plan projects performed by the KSK Consortium. She has also headed the Office since April 2018.

The Office organizes records management and document flow, including receiving, registering, and recording incoming correspondence and outgoing documents, quickly retrieving documentation, and registering business travel.

Records management at the Institute has been governed by its quality-management system since 2002. The current procedure, issued in April 2017, establishes uniform requirements for creating management documents and handling official documents.

sections.title: Core Functions
items: Organizing the Institute's records management and document flow.
Receiving, registering, and recording correspondence and outgoing documents, retrieving documentation, and registering business travel.
Maintaining uniform requirements for management and official documents under the quality-management system.

operationalTechnicalSupport.name: Halyna Slepchenko
operationalTechnicalSupport.portraitAlt: Halyna Slepchenko, Head of Operational and Technical Support for Scientific Research
biography: Halyna Slepchenko graduated from the Kyiv Technical School of Urban Electric Transport in 1980 in the construction and operation of urban and industrial transport routes.
She has experience in construction, transport, and municipal services and worked for many years as chief engineer of a municipal utility in Ivankiv.

She began working in the exclusion zone in 1988 with the Complex Expedition of the Kurchatov Institute of Atomic Energy and joined the Interdisciplinary Scientific and Technical Centre Shelter in 1992. After heading the Operations and Technical Department for many years, she became Head of Operational and Technical Support for Scientific Research at ISP NPP in January 2024.
The department supports the Institute's scientific units by providing accommodation, repairs, heat, water, electricity, and transport services.
The department has established conditions that effectively support its tasks and participates in the Institute's quality-management processes within its authority.

sections.title: Department Structure
items: Facilities Support — site manager Tetiana Denysenko.
Repair Support — site manager Fedir Cherpak.
Fitting and Mechanical Site — manager Vitalii Osnytskyi.
Transport Support — site manager Volodymyr Fedorchenko.
Facilities Support and Fitting and Mechanical Site at 12 Lysohirska Street, Kyiv — site manager Anatolii Matviichuk.

sections.title: Main Activities
items: Maintaining administrative, laboratory, production, and dormitory buildings under sanitary, radiation, fire-safety, and operating requirements.
Performing routine and cosmetic repairs in laboratory, administrative, production, and residential premises.
Repairing furniture, locks, joinery, roofs, and fencing.
Preparing buildings and premises for autumn and winter operation.
Maintaining dormitories and registering and accommodating shift workers in the exclusion zone, including bed allocation and linen exchange.
Safeguarding inventory, accounting for assets, and submitting monthly reports to the Accounting Service.
Protecting Institute buildings and maintaining the grounds.
Organizing production and manufacturing orders based on scientists' developments and the Institute's thematic plans.
Supplying premises with energy, communications, and connectivity.
Ensuring reliable, accident-free operation of energy installations, technological equipment, engineering networks, and vehicles.
Providing passenger transport to Institute departments on time.
Implementing orders, instructions, and directives from Institute management.

scientificOrganization.name: Viktoriia Havrylenko
scientificOrganization.portraitAlt: Viktoriia Havrylenko, Head of the Scientific and Organizational Department
biography: Viktoriia Havrylenko graduated from Khmelnytskyi National University in 2013 with a master's degree.

She began working at the Institute for Safety Problems of Nuclear Power Plants of the National Academy of Sciences of Ukraine in 2018 as a lead engineer in the Scientific and Organizational Department. In 2020, she became Head of the Research Coordination and International Projects Sector. She has headed the Scientific and Organizational Department since 2026.
Viktoriia Havrylenko is a scholarship holder of the Presidium of the National Academy of Sciences of Ukraine for young scientists and a co-author of more than 10 scientific articles.
directions: Monitoring the implementation of resolutions of the Presidium of the National Academy of Sciences of Ukraine and the ISP NPP Scientific Council.
Coordinating research among the Institute's scientific departments and other research institutions and organizations.
Collecting and consolidating information and preparing annual reports on the Institute's activities.
Informing scientific staff about training and professional-development courses offered by the National Academy of Sciences of Ukraine.
Coordinating the international activities of the Institute's departments.
Organizing the Institute's publishing and information activities.

problemsJournal.title: Problems of Nuclear Power Plants' Safety and of Chornobyl
problemsJournal.subtitle: A scientific journal covering fundamental and applied research into NPP safety, nuclear installations, the Shelter facility, and the environment.

metadata.label: Frequency
metadata.value: Continuing publication, up to three issues per year

metadata.label: Founder
metadata.value: Institute for Safety Problems of Nuclear Power Plants of the NAS of Ukraine

sections.title: Scope
paragraphs: Fundamental and applied NPP-safety research; scientific foundations for safe and efficient nuclear-installation operation; spent-fuel and radioactive-waste technologies; NPP decommissioning; radiation-monitoring systems and environmental and Shelter fuel-containing-material monitoring; and environmental studies around nuclear power plants and other hazardous facilities.

sections.title: Indexing and Registration
items: International Nuclear Information System (INIS).
Google Scholar.
State registration certificate KV No. 9267 of 18 October 2004.
Included in the list of Ukrainian specialized scientific publications by Ministry of Education and Science Order No. 1413 of 24 October 2017.
Fields: 101 — ecology; 143 — nuclear power engineering; 21.06.01 — environmental safety; 05.14.14 — thermal and nuclear power installations.

sections.title: Editorial Board
items: Editor-in-Chief — A. V. Nosovskyi, Corresponding Member of the NAS of Ukraine, Doctor of Technical Sciences, Professor.
Deputy Editor-in-Chief — V. M. Shcherbin, Candidate of Physical and Mathematical Sciences.
Editorial Board: L. A. Bulavin, V. I. Borysenko, S. V. Habelkov, V. M. Pavlovych, B. S. Prister, M. M. Talerko, I. H. Sharaievskyi, and Tadahiro Washiya.
Managing Editor — L. M. Troian.

sections.title: Editorial Contacts
items: 36-a Kirova Street, Chornobyl, Kyiv Region, 07270, Ukraine.
12 Lysohirska Street, Kyiv, 03028, Ukraine.
Email: l.troyan@ispnpp.kiev.ua; i.kutsyna@ispnpp.kiev.ua.

sections.title: Role of Peer Review
paragraphs: Peer review is an essential part of modern science. Detailed examination of methods and results helps determine the best direction for future research. Work accepted after peer review meets established scientific standards.
The editorial office uses peer review to publish high-quality material and maintain readers' trust.
items: More reliable: reviewers identify gaps requiring explanation or additional experiments.
Easier to understand: unclear passages are corrected.
More useful: the research is assessed for importance within its field.

sections.title: Why Review
items: Contribute to science by objectively assessing other researchers' methods.
Build professional experience that strengthens an academic CV.
Learn about recent developments before publication.
Develop critical-thinking skills useful in one's own research and writing.

sections.title: Reviewing Perspectives
items: Authors: review the manuscript as one would want one's own work reviewed and provide useful comments even when recommending rejection.
Editor: assess relevance, novelty, scientific quality, and ways to improve the manuscript.
Readers: identify passages needing clarification and remove errors and nonessential material.

sections.title: Before Accepting
items: Confirm suitable expertise for the full manuscript or tell the editor which parts can be assessed.
Confirm sufficient time to meet the deadline without delaying publication.
Disclose potential financial, professional, or personal conflicts of interest.

sections.title: Ethical Principles
paragraphs: Manuscripts under review are strictly confidential. Reviewers must not discuss them with third parties without the editor's approval or use information for personal benefit.
A report should improve the work: avoid personal or excessively negative comments, identify strengths, and propose specific solutions.

sections.title: Reviewer Procedure
items: Articles may undergo review by the Editor-in-Chief, open external review, single-blind review, or double-blind review. Work by master's students and early-career degree candidates also requires a departmental or supervisor recommendation.
The Editor-in-Chief selects the type of review.
A reviewer must accept or give a reasoned refusal within three working days; review may not exceed two weeks.
The review assesses theoretical or applied significance, author contribution, style, logic, accessibility, reliability, and justification of conclusions.
The Editorial Board makes the final publication decision; reviewers remain anonymous when publication is refused.
The Editorial Board may request additional anonymous external review.
Positive reviews alone do not guarantee publication; the final decision belongs to the Editorial Board.
Original reviews are retained by the editorial office.
Material receiving substantial criticism but an overall positive recommendation may be published as a discussion paper.

sections.title: Manuscript Submission
items: Previously unpublished articles within the journal's scope and with scientific and practical value are accepted.
Article text and supporting material must be carefully edited, checked, and signed by the authors.
Submit in English, Ukrainian, or Russian together with a single Microsoft Word file.

sections.title: Electronic Format
items: A4 paper; margins: top 2.0 cm, bottom 2.5 cm, right 1.0 cm, left 3.0 cm.
Times New Roman, 1.5 line spacing, 1.25 cm paragraph indent.
No more than six clear black-and-white figures and photographs inserted in the text; replace labels with letter or number keys where possible and explain them in captions.
Use MathType for equations. Tables must have titles and be understandable without referring to the main text.

sections.title: Article Structure
items: UDC index.
Article title and authors' initials and surnames.
Abstract and keywords in Ukrainian, Russian, and English.
Problem statement, review of research, objective, main material, results, conclusions, and prospects for further research.
References in citation order.
Full author information: name, degree, title, position, institution, postal address, telephone, fax, and email.

sections.title: English Titles and Abstracts
items: Titles must be informative and use only widely accepted abbreviations.
Do not transliterate Ukrainian words except proper names and untranslatable named objects; avoid local professional slang.
The English abstract must be concise, informative, original, substantive, and structured, with at least 1,000 characters including the title, authors, and no more than five keywords.
Use natural English scientific terminology, active voice, and consistent terminology.
The abstract must stand alone, avoid repeating the title, and omit unnecessary introductions and generally known statements.

sections.title: Names, Institutions, and References
items: Render Ukrainian surnames in Latin characters under Cabinet of Ministers Resolution No. 55 of 27 January 2010.
Capitalize all significant words in English institution names except articles and prepositions.
Provide a complete separate Latin-script References section repeating the source-language bibliography.
For Cyrillic sources, include transliteration, an English title translation, and the original language.
Include a publication's DOI in the reference list when available.

qualityPolicyPage.title: Quality Policy
qualityPolicyPage.intro: The Institute for Safety Problems of Nuclear Power Plants of the National Academy of Sciences of Ukraine is a state research organization. Its principal activities are defined by the Presidium of the NAS of Ukraine and concern the development of applied research in the use of nuclear energy.

directions.title: Scientific Areas
items: Transformation of the Shelter object into an environmentally safe system.
Operational safety of nuclear installations.
Decommissioning of nuclear installations.
Management of spent nuclear fuel and radioactive waste.

mission.title: Mission
mission.description: To create, acquire, develop, disseminate, and apply scientific knowledge and advanced technologies for the safe use of nuclear energy and for preventing and mitigating the consequences of radiation accidents for the benefit of society.

vision.title: Vision
vision.description: The Institute aims to become a leading Ukrainian research institution providing scientific, technical, engineering, methodological, and information services for the safe operation of nuclear-technology facilities, radiation-accident recovery, decommissioning, and radioactive-waste and spent-fuel management, with national and international standing.

implementation.title: Policy Implementation
implementation.intro: The quality policy is implemented through:
items: Continuous and systematic quality management at every stage of scientific and production activity.
Research and development in accredited laboratories with continuous internal control of results.
Compliance with requirements and continual improvement of QMS effectiveness under ISO 9001:2015.
An interconnected process system ensuring complete fulfillment of customer and stakeholder requirements.
Systematic staff training and professional development.

principles.title: Core Principles
principles.intro: High-quality, competitive products and scientific and technical services are delivered according to these principles:
items: Nuclear and radiation safety is the highest priority.
Environmental safety of production is ensured.
Top management is personally responsible for prioritizing the resources needed to meet safety requirements.
The scientific and material infrastructure is continually improved.
Work is performed by highly qualified professionals.
Conditions support continuous staff development, professional growth, and the realization of creative potential.
Mutually beneficial relations with partners and suppliers, continual fulfillment of customer requirements, and prompt response to changing needs.

qualityPolicyPage.commitment: Institute personnel, from the Director to every employee, form a team committed to implementing the quality policy. ISP NPP top management accepts responsibility for its implementation, timely provision of required resources, and continual improvement of the quality management system.
qualityPolicyPage.backToQms: Back to the Quality Management System

qmsPage.title: Quality Management System
qmsPage.subtitle: ISP NPP of the NAS of Ukraine has developed, implemented, and maintains a quality management system compliant with the ISO 9001 international standard and regulatory requirements for the use of nuclear energy.
qmsPage.certification: The QMS is certified by Bureau Veritas Quality International as compliant with ISO 9001:2015.

scope.title: Scope
scope.description: The Institute’s quality management system covers design, development, and service delivery in the field of nuclear-energy use. It includes leadership, resource provision, product life-cycle, analysis, measurement, and continual-improvement processes.

leadership.title: System Leadership
leadership.description: Quality-management duties are assigned to managers at every level. Overall management is exercised by the Institute Director. Top management is responsible for quality planning, resources, QMS improvement, and oversight of cooperation among the Institute’s units.

principles.title: Organization of QMS Processes
items: The core processes required for the QMS have been defined and implemented throughout the organization.
Duties, authority, responsibility, reporting lines, and interaction procedures have been established for process owners and performers.
The sequence and interaction of processes have been established.
Assessment criteria and methods have been defined to ensure effective process operation and control.
Resources and information required to operate and monitor processes are provided.
Process monitoring, measurement, and analysis are maintained.
Measures are taken to achieve planned results and continually improve processes.
Controls address nonconformities, emergency prevention, and mitigation of emergency consequences.

responsibilities.title: Process Responsibilities
responsibilities.intro: Responsible managers and units are assigned to the key QMS processes.

items.owner: Head of the Division for Design of Facilities with Radiation and Nuclear Technologies

items.process: Creation of scientific and technical products

items.owner: Head of the Quality Management Sector, Radiation Safety, Quality and Occupational Safety Department
Head of the Quality Management Sector, Radiation Safety, Quality and Occupational Safety Department

documentation.title: QMS Documentation
documentation.intro: In accordance with ISO 9001:2015, the Institute’s quality management documentation includes:
items: A documented quality policy.
Documented quality objectives for the Institute’s units.
The quality manual.
Quality programs for activities subject to licensing in the field of nuclear-energy use.
Quality programs and special procedures for work under international contracts.
Documented procedures, measurement methods, enterprise standards, external and customer documentation, and other documents needed for process control and compliant output.
Quality records.

nuclearPowerJournal.title: Nuclear Power and the Environment
nuclearPowerJournal.subtitle: A professional journal devoted to the safe use of nuclear technologies, the development of nuclear power, and the protection of people and the environment.
nuclearPowerJournal.officialSiteHint: Current issues, author guidelines, and full-text articles are available on the journal’s official website.
nuclearPowerJournal.coverAlt: Cover of the Nuclear Power and the Environment journal

about.title: About the Journal
about.description: The journal publishes open results of theoretical and applied research of scientific and practical interest to researchers, engineers, lecturers, students, postgraduate researchers, and nuclear-industry professionals.
about.purpose: Its purpose is to present new technical solutions, nuclear-installation safety assessment methods, and research findings in nuclear power, public protection, and environmental protection.

scope.title: Journal Scope
items: General nuclear-power issues
Safety of nuclear installations
Spent nuclear fuel and radioactive waste
Decommissioning of nuclear installations
The Shelter object
Environmental safety

status.title: Professional Status
status.description: The journal is included in category B of Ukraine’s list of professional scientific publications. Its subject areas include 101 Environmental Science, 126 Information Systems and Technologies, and 143 Nuclear Power Engineering.
status.cluster: It belongs to the Safe, Clean Energy and Energy Efficiency cluster and the G4 Energy Production specialty.

founders.title: Co-founders
items: Institute for Safety Problems of Nuclear Power Plants of the NAS of Ukraine
Ukrainian Nuclear Society
JSC National Nuclear Energy Generating Company Energoatom

access.title: Open Access
access.description: The journal follows an open-access policy. Full-text articles are available through the archive on its official website.
